---
title: "automatedRecLin"
subtitle: "Record Linkage Based on an Entropy-Maximizing Classifier"
date: July 8, 2026
date-format: "[useR! Conference, Warsaw, Poland<br>]MMMM D, YYYY"
format:
  revealjs:
    transition: fade
bibliography: bibl.bib
csl: apa.csl
author:
  - name: Adam Struzik
    email: adastr5@st.amu.edu.pl
    orcid: 0009-0002-2547-482X
    affiliation:
      - ref: amu
      - ref: urban
  - name: Maciej Beręsewicz
    email: maciej.beresewicz@ue.poznan.pl
    orcid: 0000-0002-8281-4301
    affiliation:
      - ref: pueb
      - ref: pop
affiliations:
  - id: amu
    name: Faculty of Mathematics and Computer Science, Adam Mickiewicz University in Poznań, Poland
  - id: urban
    name: Center for Urban Statistics, Statistical Office in Poznań, Poland
  - id: pueb
    name: Department of Statistics, Poznań University of Economics and Business, Poland
  - id: pop
    name: Center for the Methodology of Population Studies, Statistical Office in Poznań, Poland
---

## Problem

> **Record linkage** --- identifying records that refer to the same entity across two datasets. 

::: columns
::: {.column width = "45%" style = "font-size: 70%;"}
**Dataset A**

| name | surname | city |
|------|---------|------|
| [Emily]{.fragment .highlight-blue data-fragment-index="1"} | [Johnson]{.fragment .highlight-blue data-fragment-index="1"} | [Chicago]{.fragment .highlight-blue data-fragment-index="1"} |
| [Michael]{.fragment .highlight-red data-fragment-index="1"} | [Anderson]{.fragment .highlight-red data-fragment-index="1"} | [Seattle]{.fragment .highlight-red data-fragment-index="1"} |
| [Sarah]{.fragment .highlight-green data-fragment-index="1"} | [Thompson]{.fragment .highlight-green data-fragment-index="1"} | [Boston]{.fragment .highlight-green data-fragment-index="1"} |
:::
::: {.column width = "45%" style = "font-size: 70%;"}
**Dataset B**

| name | surname | city |
|------|---------|------|
| [Sara]{.fragment .highlight-green data-fragment-index="1"} | [Tompson]{.fragment .highlight-green data-fragment-index="1"} | [Boston]{.fragment .highlight-green data-fragment-index="1"} |
| [Emily]{.fragment .highlight-blue data-fragment-index="1"} | [Johnson]{.fragment .highlight-blue data-fragment-index="1"} | [Chicago]{.fragment .highlight-blue data-fragment-index="1"} |
| [Michael]{.fragment .highlight-red data-fragment-index="1"} | [Andersson]{.fragment .highlight-red data-fragment-index="1"} | [Seatle]{.fragment .highlight-red data-fragment-index="1"} |
:::
:::

::: {.fragment data-fragment-index="1"}
- There are no shared unique identifiers.
- Names, surnames, and locations may contain typos and discrepancies.
:::

## Method

```{r}
#| include: false
library(automatedRecLin)
jw <- jarowinkler_complement()
```

- Starting point: **maximum entropy classification (MEC)** for record linkage [@lee2022].
- Original idea: compare records using agreement/disagreement (binary) indicators.
- Our extension: use **continuous comparison functions** instead.

::: {style = "font-size: 70%;"}
| values | binary comparison | continuous comparison |
|--------|-------------------|:---------------------:|
| "Johnson" vs "Johnson" | agreement | `r sprintf("%.3f", jw("Johnson", "Johnson"))` |
| "Anderson" vs "Andersson" | disagreement | `r sprintf("%.3f", jw("Anderson", "Andersson"))` |
| "Seattle" vs "Seatle" | disagreement | `r sprintf("%.3f", jw("Seattle", "Seatle"))` |
:::
::: {style = "font-size: 50%;"}
Here, the continuous comparison function is 1 $-$ Jaro-Winkler similarity [@Winkler1990]: 0 means identical values; larger values indicate stronger disagreement.
:::

## The [`automatedRecLin`](https://cran.r-project.org/package=automatedRecLin) Package

- Implements the approach by @lee2022, as well as its continuous parametric [cf. @Vo2023] and nonparametric [cf. @sugiyama2008] extensions.
- Provides **fully automatic** record linkage, both supervised and unsupervised.
- Enables integration with the [`blocking`](https://cran.r-project.org/package=blocking) package [@blocking] via the [`mec_blocking()`](https://ncn-foreigners.ue.poznan.pl/automatedRecLin/reference/mec_blocking.html) function, reducing the computational complexity of the process.
- Available on [CRAN](https://cran.r-project.org/package=automatedRecLin).

```{r}
#| include: false
library(readr)
library(data.table)

set.seed(42)

data(census)
data(cis)
prd <- read_csv("data-raw/prd.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

setDT(census)
setDT(cis)
prd <- as.data.frame(lapply(prd, as.character))
prd[is.na(prd)] <- ""
colnames(prd) <- tolower(colnames(prd))
setDT(prd)

census[, pername1 := gsub("-", "", pername1)]
cis[, pername1 := gsub("-", "", pername1)]
prd[, pername1 := gsub("-", "", pername1)]

n_A <- 500
n_B <- 1000
ids <- Reduce(intersect, list(census$person_id, prd$person_id, cis$person_id))

p_A <- 0.8
ids_prd <- sample(ids, n_A)
matched_individ <- sample(ids_prd, n_A * p_A)
cis_filtered <- cis[sex != "" & dob_year != "" & dob_mon != ""]
cis_filtered <- cis[sex == "F" & as.numeric(dob_year) <= 1970 & (as.numeric(dob_mon) %% 2) == 0]
ids_cis <- sample(setdiff(cis_filtered$person_id, ids_prd), n_B - n_A * p_A)
ids_cis <- c(matched_individ, ids_cis)

df1 <- prd[person_id %in% ids_prd, ]
df2 <- cis[person_id %in% ids_cis, ]

matches <- merge(x = df1[, .(x=1:.N, person_id)],
                 y = df2[, .(y = 1:.N, person_id)],
                 by = "person_id")
setnames(matches, c("x", "y"), c("a", "b"))
set(matches, j = "person_id", value = NULL)
```

## Interface {auto-animate=true}

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
mec(
  A = df1,
  B = df2,
  variables = c(
    "pername1", "pername2", "sex",
    "dob_day", "dob_mon", "dob_year"
  )
)
```

::: {style = "font-size: 50%;"}
Unsupervised MEC with binary comparisons on all key variables.
:::

## Interface {auto-animate=true}

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
mec(
  A = df1,
  B = df2,
  variables = c(
    "pername1", "pername2", "sex",
    "dob_day", "dob_mon", "dob_year"
  ),
  comparators = list(
    "pername1" = jarowinkler_complement(),
    "pername2" = jarowinkler_complement()
  ),
  methods = list(
    "pername1" = "continuous_parametric",
    "pername2" = "continuous_parametric"
  )
)
```

::: {style = "font-size: 50%;"}
Unsupervised MEC with continuous comparisons (1 $-$ Jaro-Winkler similarity) on name and surname. We use the parametric approach.
:::

```{r}
#| include: false

rl_result_b <- mec(
  A = df1,
  B = df2,
  variables = c(
    "pername1", "pername2", "sex",
    "dob_day", "dob_mon", "dob_year"
  ),
  true_matches = matches
)

rl_result_cpar <- mec(
  A = df1,
  B = df2,
  variables = c(
    "pername1", "pername2", "sex",
    "dob_day", "dob_mon", "dob_year"
  ),
  comparators = list(
    "pername1" = jarowinkler_complement(),
    "pername2" = jarowinkler_complement()
  ),
  methods = list(
    "pername1" = "continuous_parametric",
    "pername2" = "continuous_parametric"
  ),
  true_matches = matches
)
```

## Example Results

```{r}
library(knitr)

metrics_b <- rl_result_b$eval_metrics
metrics_cpar <- rl_result_cpar$eval_metrics

metrics <- data.table(
  "Method" = c("Binary", "Continuous parametric"),
  "No. of links" = integer(2),
  "False Linkage Rate" = numeric(2),
  "Missing Match Rate" = numeric(2)
)

metrics[["No. of links"]] <- c(
  NROW(rl_result_b$M_est),
  NROW(rl_result_cpar$M_est)
)
metrics[["False Linkage Rate"]] <- sprintf(
  "%.4f",
  c(
    metrics_b[1],
    metrics_cpar[1]
  )
)
metrics[["Missing Match Rate"]] <- sprintf(
  "%.4f",
  c(
    metrics_b[2],
    metrics_cpar[2]
  )
)

kable(metrics)
```

::: {style = "font-size: 50%;"}
The true number of matches is 400. Note that False Linkage Rate is equal to 1 $-$ Precision, and Missing Match Rate is equal to 1 $-$ Recall.
:::

::: {.fragment}
Observations:

- Continuous comparisons improve error rates.
- They effectively capture small discrepancies between records.
:::

## 

[automatedRecLin]{.r-fit-text}

![](qrcode_github.com.png){fig-align="center"}

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
